title: Glaze trailing on a flat stoneware plate
subtitle: Trailing liquid glaze in freehand lines on bisqued stoneware
excerpt: Liquid glaze is loaded into a slip trailer and drawn across the surface of a bisqued flat stoneware plate in lines, curves, or drawn patterns. The trailed glaze stands slightly proud of the surface before firing and fuses into a raised line in the finished piece.
sourceNotes: Glaze trailing on flat stoneware technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Ceramic work through a first low-temperature firing to harden it without glaze. Bisqueware is porous and ready to accept glaze.
Drawing lines of liquid glaze directly on bisqueware from a squeeze bottle or rubber bulb. Related to traditional slip trailing but using glaze rather than slip.
A rubber bulb or plastic squeeze bottle with a narrow nozzle for drawing precise lines of liquid clay or glaze on a surface.

content.text: loaded with liquid glaze to draw directly on

. A flat plate is the ideal starting surface: the banding wheel keeps the plate rotating under the trailer nozzle for concentric rings, or you can work freehand with the wheel stationary for drawn designs. The trailed glaze builds up slightly above the surface and retains a gentle raised line through firing.

attrs.content: Use pre-mixed commercial glaze only. Wear an N95 mask when handling glaze. Clean up wet — never dry sweep. Dedicate the slip trailer to ceramic use only.

items.name: Pre-mixed mid-fire glaze (thickened slightly for trailing)
Slip trailer (rubber bulb or plastic squeeze bottle)
Pottery brush, mop (for background coat if needed)

content.text: For trailing, the glaze should be slightly thicker than dipping consistency, about the texture of double cream. If it drips freely from the nozzle without prompting, thicken it by leaving it exposed for 10 minutes before filling the trailer. Load the slip trailer by squeezing it to expel air, dipping the nozzle into the glaze, and releasing to draw glaze in. Test the flow on a spare piece of bisqueware before applying to the plate.

Place the plate on the banding wheel. Set the wheel spinning slowly and hold the slip trailer nozzle 10-15 mm above the plate surface, angled slightly inward. Allow the glaze to trail out in a continuous line as the wheel turns. Move the trailer nozzle gradually from the rim toward the centre to produce a spiral or from fixed positions to produce discrete rings. The line should be 2-3 mm wide and 1-2 mm proud of the surface.

For a freehand design, leave the banding wheel stationary. Hold the trailer 10 mm above the surface and draw with a continuous motion, as if drawing with a pen. Simple flowing curves, leaf shapes, and loose botanical outlines work best. Complex fine detail tends to lose definition during firing. Work quickly and do not go back to correct mistakes while the glaze is wet; allow it to dry and scrape off any errors with a knife before re-trailing.
Apply a background coat of a contrasting base glaze by brushing first, then trail a darker or lighter glaze on top once the base is dry.
Use two different glaze colours in the trailer (one on each half) for a two-tone trailing that blends where the colours meet.

items.symptom: Trailed lines spread flat during firing with no raised texture
items.cause: Glaze too runny; insufficient clay content to hold its form
items.fix: Thicken the trailing glaze slightly before filling the trailer. A glaze with higher clay content (kaolin or ball clay) retains its shape better

items.cause: Glaze particles settling in the nozzle; nozzle diameter too small for the glaze particle size
items.fix: Use a trailer with a 3-4 mm nozzle diameter. Sieve the glaze through a fine-mesh sieve before loading the trailer
